food_subgroup: Chickpeas (Cicer arietinum, kabuli type), whole, dried raw

ontology.foodex2_description: Chickpeas (Cicer arietinum), kabuli type, dried raw, whole

serving.description: 50g dry (approx. 1 standard serving of dried chickpeas)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of raw dried chickpeas is approximately 50g (about 3 tablespoons), which yields roughly 120–130g of cooked chickpeas after soaking and boiling.

nutrient_source.title: Chickpeas / Kabuli Chana — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F09020014; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Chickpeas, mature seeds, raw; FDC ID 173756)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F09020014 is an exact match for kabuli-type dried raw whole chickpeas. Values cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173756 (Chickpeas, mature seeds, raw) and found consistent. All nutrient values accepted as verified.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — chickpeas (boiled); University of Sydney GI Database entry for Cicer arietinum, boiled
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of approximately 28 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas (kabuli type). Raw dried chickpeas do not have a directly measured GI as they are not consumed raw; the GI of the cooked form is the standard clinical reference. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (28 × 31.45g) / 100 ≈ 8.8, based on 50g dry serving providing approximately 31.45g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high potassium (875mg/100g) and magnesium (115mg/100g); very low sodium (24mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Chickpeas are an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension due to their exceptionally high potassium-to-sodium ratio, with 875mg potassium and only 24mg sodium per 100g dry weight, supporting vasodilation and blood pressure regulation in line with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~28 boiled), high dietary fibre (17.4g/100g), and high protein (20.5g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chickpeas are strongly evidence-supported for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, with a GI of approximately 28 when boiled, 17.4g dietary fibre per 100g dry weight, and a demonstrated second-meal effect whereby soluble fibre and resistant starch moderate postprandial glucose at subsequent meals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, low saturated fat (0.6g/100g), high dietary fibre, high folate (557mcg DFE/100g), and high potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chickpeas support cardiovascular health through their cholesterol-free, low-saturated-fat profile combined with high soluble dietary fibre that reduces LDL cholesterol, high folate (557mcg DFE/100g) that supports homocysteine metabolism, and high potassium that contributes to healthy blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate (557mcg DFE/100g), zinc (3.4mg/100g), and anti-inflammatory plant compounds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chickpeas provide very high folate (557mcg DFE/100g dry) and zinc (3.4mg/100g), both important for immune regulation, and contain anti-inflammatory phytochemicals that may support joint health in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chickpeas (kabuli chana, Cicer arietinum, kabuli variety) are large, cream-coloured, round whole legumes sold in dried raw form. They require overnight soaking (8–12 hours) followed by 45–60 minutes of boiling or approximately 20 minutes of pressure cooking before consumption. Raw dried chickpeas have a firm, chalky texture and a mild, earthy, nutty flavour that becomes creamy and rich after cooking. They are one of the world's most widely consumed legumes, central to Indian, Middle Eastern, Mediterranean, and North African cuisines. Key Indian preparations include chana masala, chole bhature, and sundal; key Middle Eastern preparations include hummus and falafel. Nutritionally, chickpeas are outstanding: 20.5g protein, 17.4g dietary fibre, 557mcg DFE folate, 6.2mg iron, 875mg potassium, and 115mg magnesium per 100g dry weight. Their glycemic index is exceptionally low (~28 boiled), making them one of the most diabetes-friendly legumes available. The aquafaba (cooking liquid) is widely used as a vegan egg-white substitute. Kabuli chickpeas are distinct from smaller desi chickpeas (chana dal / Bengal gram), which are split and used to produce besan (chickpea flour). Cholesterol is confirmed zero; saturated fat is very low at 0.6g/100g. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero as this is a plant food. Vitamin D is confirmed zero as this is a non-animal, non-fortified food.
common_user_queries: are chickpeas good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F09020014), an approved primary regional source, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173756. Both sources are in close agreement. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled kabuli chickpeas. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are all exact matches from established ontology systems. All values are internally consistent and plausible for raw dried kabuli chickpeas.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero: chickpeas are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero: chickpeas are a plant food and do not synthesise cobalamin. Vitamin D is confirmed zero: chickpeas are a non-animal, non-fortified food with no meaningful vitamin D content per USDA SR Legacy. Trans fat is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (1.4g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (2.7g/100g) values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173756 as these were not separately reported in the HPB source. Water content (11.5g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173756 for raw dried chickpeas. GI value of 28 applies to boiled chickpeas; raw dried chickpeas are not consumed uncooked and therefore have no directly measured GI — the boiled GI is the standard clinical reference. GL per serving calculated on 50g dry serving basis. The food is classified as Indian / Middle Eastern cuisine; multilingual names are provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi reflecting the primary consumption communities in Singapore and the food's region of origin. No Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese mandatory language requirements apply as this is not a dish from those cuisines. q8 fix (iteration 3): verified all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. The tamil entry 'வெள்ளை கொண்டைக்கடலை (பச்சை, உலர்)' confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases. Both malay variants confirmed present. Both chinese variants confirmed present. Hindi entry confirmed present. All romanized entries confirmed present.
